description: Parmenides' influence on Plato is foundational. The Theory of Forms is essentially a Parmenidean move: true reality (the Forms) is eternal, unchanging, and knowable only by reason — just as Parmenides' Being is eternal, unchanging, and accessible only to rational thought rather than sense experience. Plato even wrote a dialogue called 'Parmenides' in which Socrates debates the historical Parmenides — an unusual act of philosophical tribute and self-examination. The key difference: Plato accepted the reality of the changing world as a lower grade of reality (participation), where Parmenides dismissed it entirely as illusion.
